children.name: Cacatua
children.description: White cockatoos — medium to large parrots with white plumage and prominent erectile crests. Found across Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesia. Highly intelligent and social, they form large, noisy flocks. Their powerful bills can crack the hardest seeds and nuts. Several species are popular in aviculture.

children.name: Cacatua galerita
children.description: A large white cockatoo with a brilliant yellow crest. Widespread across Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesian islands. Highly intelligent, long-lived (up to 80 years in captivity), and famous for its loud, raucous call. In Australia it is both beloved and considered a pest in urban areas.

children.name: Cacatua moluccensis
children.description: Endemic to the Seram archipelago in Indonesia. Salmon-pink crest and body with a powerful beak. One of the largest white cockatoos. Classified as Vulnerable due to illegal trapping for the pet trade.

children.name: Cacatua goffiniana
children.description: A small white cockatoo from the Tanimbar Islands of Indonesia. Known for remarkable problem-solving abilities. A feral population established in Singapore. Named after the wife of Dutch ornithologist Dirk Goffin.

children.name: Eolophus
children.description: The galah — a monotypic genus containing one of Australia's most familiar and abundant cockatoos. Distinctive pink-and-grey plumage makes it unmistakable. Highly social, forming large flocks that can number thousands.

children.name: Eolophus roseicapilla
children.description: One of Australia's most ubiquitous birds. Rose-pink chest and head, grey wings and back, with a pale pink crest. Found across most of Australia in open country. Flocks can number in the thousands. The name 'galah' is also Australian slang for a fool.

children.name: Callocephalon
children.description: The gang-gang cockatoo — a monotypic genus endemic to southeastern Australia. The male is unmistakable with its wispy, forward-curving crimson crest that resembles a feathery toupee. The species has a distinctive creaking call like a rusty gate.

children.name: Callocephalon fimbriatum
children.description: Endemic to southeastern Australia. Male has a grey body and a spectacular red wispy crest; female is grey with barred underparts. Makes a distinctive creaking call like an unoiled gate. The faunal emblem of the Australian Capital Territory.

children.name: Nymphicus
children.description: The cockatiel — a monotypic genus and the smallest member of the cockatoo family. Separated from the true cockatoos by its long tail and more slender build. One of the most popular pet birds worldwide, renowned for its whistling ability and gentle temperament.

children.name: Nymphicus hollandicus
children.description: The only member of its genus and the smallest cockatoo. Grey body, yellow head, and orange cheek patches. Native to the Australian outback, where it travels in nomadic flocks. One of the most popular cage birds globally, with many colour mutations developed in captivity.
